result:
Provenance complete: 27 journal-admissible works + 2 flagged non-admissible leads, every DOI registry-verified this session, deduped against the 10-work seed, nothing fetched or bound. Returning the payload (also persisted at /home/polaris/polaris_project/outputs/acquisition_campaign/canon_and_contrasts_leads.json).

(Relevance note: more productivity- than employment-focused — admit for the mechanism, not as direct labor evidence.)","what_it_contrasts_with":"Adjustment-lag reconciles weak short-run effects (X7, X8) with long-run transformation — a temporal rather than sign contrast."},

    {"id":"X2","role":"contrast","doi":"10.1093/jeea/jvab012","doi_verified":true,"title":"The Adjustment of Labor Markets to Robots","authors":"Dauth, Wolfgang; Findeisen, Sebastian; Suedekum, Jens; Woessner, Nicole","venue":"Journal of the European Economic Association","type":"journal","year":2021,"volume":"19(6)","pages":"3104-3153","admissible_task72":true,"why_relevant":"German worker-level data: robots destroy manufacturing jobs but create ~as many in services — NO net employment loss; incidence on labor share and young entrants.","what_it_contrasts_with":"Opposite NET-employment finding to Acemoglu-Restrepo US (C1); different country (Germany), individual-level data — a geography/institution contrast."},
    {"id":"X3","role":"contrast","doi":"10.1162/rest_a_01083","doi_verified":true,"title":"Benign Effects of Automation: New Evidence from Patent Texts","authors":"Mann, Katja; Puttmann, Lukas","venue":"Review of Economics and Statistics","type":"journal","year":2023,"volume":"105(3)","pages":"562-579","admissible_task72":true,"why_relevant":"Classifies 1976-2014 US patents from text; automation innovation RAISES local employment, driven by services.","what_it_contrasts_with":"OPPOSITE SIGN to Acemoglu-Restrepo robots (C1); SAME patent-text method as Webb (W1) but benign conclusion — method-shared, opposite-result contrast."},
    {"id":"X4","role":"contrast","doi":"10.1093/jeea/jvab040","doi_verified":true,"title":"Racing With or Against the Machine? Evidence on the Role of Trade in Europe","authors":"Gregory, Terry; Salomons, Anna; Zierahn, Ulrich","venue":"Journal of the European Economic Association","type":"journal","year":2022,"volume":"20(2)","pages":"869-906","admissible_task72":true,"why_relevant":"Europe (27 countries): routine-replacing technical change produced NET POSITIVE labor demand once product-demand and spillovers are counted.","what_it_contrasts_with":"Net-positive vs displacement (C1); operationalizes Autor's complementarity view (C7) cross-country."},
    {"id":"X5","role":"contrast","doi":"10.1093/epolic/eiaa001","doi_verified":true,"title":"Automation and jobs: when technology boosts employment","authors":"Bessen, James","venue":"Economic Policy","type":"journal","year":2019,"volume":"34(100)","pages":"589-626","admissible_task72":true,"why_relevant":"Employment sign hinges on DEMAND ELASTICITY: automation can boost employment when demand is elastic (textiles, steel, autos) and cut it when demand is satiated.","what_it_contrasts_with":"Conditions the displacement result — a mechanism-level contrast to fixed-demand models (C1). Note: published 2019, not 2020."},
    {"id":"X6","role":"contrast","doi":"10.1353/eca.2018.0000","doi_verified":true,"title":"Is Automation Labor-Displacing? Productivity Growth, Employment, and the Labor Share","authors":"Autor, David H.; Salomons, Anna","venue":"Brookings Papers on Economic Activity","type":"conference_journal","year":2018,"volume":"2018(1) Spring","pages":"1-87","admissible_task72":true,"why_relevant":"Cross-country/industry: automation has NOT been net employment-displacing in aggregate, but HAS reduced labor's share of value added.","what_it_contrasts_with":"Splits the verdict — null on employment, negative on labor share — nuance against both pure-pessimist and pure-optimist reads.","provenance_note":"BPEA is a peer-reviewed conference journal (ISSN 0007-2303, has DOIs) — generally journal-attributable, but CONFIRM against the task-72 venue whitelist; NBER w24871 twin — do not bind the WP."},
    {"id":"X7","role":"contrast","doi":"10.1086/718327","doi_verified":true,"title":"Artificial Intelligence and Jobs: Evidence from Online Vacancies","authors":"Acemoglu, Daron; Autor, David; Hazell, Jonathon; Restrepo, Pascual","venue":"Journal of Labor Economics","type":"journal","year":2022,"volume":"40(S1)","pages":"S293-S340","admissible_task72":true,"why_relevant":"First establishment-level AI-hiring evidence (Burning Glass vacancies): AI-exposed establishments cut non-AI hiring and restructure skills, but NO detectable AGGREGATE employment effect yet.","what_it_contrasts_with":"Muted/near-null AGGREGATE AI-employment result — and the SAME lead authors as the robot-displacement work (C1) finding a weaker AI effect. Counters alarmist narratives.","provenance_note":"NBER w28257 twin — bind the JOLE version."},
    {"id":"X8","role":"contrast","doi":"10.3389/frai.2022.832736","doi_verified":true,"title":"Artificial Intelligence and Employment: New Cross-Country Evidence","authors":"Georgieff, Alexandre; Hyee, Raphaela","venue":"Frontiers in Artificial Intelligence","type":"journal","year":2022,"volume":"5","pages":"832736","admissible_task72":true,"why_relevant":"23 OECD countries: NO negative association between AI exposure and employment; POSITIVE where computer use/skills are high.","what_it_contrasts_with":"Cross-country null-to-positive AI-employment result; contrasts displacement expectations (C1, C8).","provenance_note":"BIND the Frontiers in AI 2022 journal article (makes an otherwise WP-only finding admissible). Do NOT bind the OECD SEM WP No. 265 (10.1787/c2c1d276-en)."},
    {"id":"G1","role":"generative_ai_turn","doi":"10.1126/science.adj0998","doi_verified":true,"title":"GPTs are GPTs: Labor market impact potential of LLMs","authors":"Eloundou, Tyna; Manning, Sam; Mishkin, Pamela; Rock, Daniel","venue":"Science","type":"journal","year":2024,"volume":"384(6702)","pages":"1306-1308","admissible_task72":true,"why_relevant":"First large occupation-exposure map for LLMs: ~46% of jobs could have >half their tasks exposed with complementary software; higher-wage jobs MORE exposed. Fills the corpus's missing post-2023 generative-AI turn.","what_it_contrasts_with":"Generative-AI exposure skews HIGH-wage/white-collar — flips the robot/routine (C1) and Frey-Osborne (C8) gradients. Current-evidence anchor the corpus (ends 2023) lacks.","provenance_note":"BIND Science 2024 10.1126/science.adj0998, NOT the 2023 arXiv preprint 2303.10130 (the '80%/10%' version)."},
    {"id":"G2","role":"generative_ai_turn","doi":"10.1126/science.adh2586","doi_verified":true,"title":"Experimental evidence on the productivity effects of generative artificial intelligence","authors":"Noy, Shakked; Zhang, Whitney","venue":"Science","type":"journal","year":2023,"volume":"381(6654)","pages":"187-192","admissible_task72":true,"why_relevant":"RCT (453 professionals): ChatGPT cut task time ~40%, raised quality ~18%, and COMPRESSED the skill distribution (larger gains for lower-ability workers). Current causal generative-AI evidence.","what_it_contrasts_with":"Causal experiment vs observational exposure indices (C13, G1); skill-COMPRESSION contrasts SBTC (C9)."},
    {"id":"G3","role":"generative_ai_turn","doi":"10.1093/qje/qjae044","doi_verified":true,"title":"Generative AI at Work","authors":"Brynjolfsson, Erik; Li, Danielle; Raymond, Lindsey R.","venue":"Quarterly Journal of Economics","type":"journal","year":2025,"volume":"140(2)","pages":"889-942","admissible_task72":true,"why_relevant":"Field evidence (5,172 customer-support agents): generative-AI assistant raised productivity ~15%, largest gains for less-experienced/lower-skill workers; diffuses tacit knowledge. Flagship real-world generative-AI-and-labor journal paper.","what_it_contrasts_with":"Skill-LEVELING contrasts the SBTC canon (C9); real-world complement to Noy-Zhang's lab result (G2).","provenance_note":"BIND QJE 2025 10.1093/qje/qjae044, NOT NBER WP w31161."},
    {"id":"W1","role":"lead_flagged","doi":"10.2139/ssrn.3482150","doi_verified":true,"title":"The Impact of Artificial Intelligence on the Labor Market","authors":"Webb, Michael","venue":"SSRN Working Paper No. 3482150 (Stanford)","type":"working_paper","year":2019,"admissible_task72":false,"why_relevant":"The named 'Webb'. Patent-text vs job-task overlap; AI exposure concentrated among HIGH-skill occupations; predicts AI narrows 90:10 inequality (not the top 1%). Cornerstone of the AI-exposure literature.","what_it_contrasts_with":"High-skill AI exposure contrasts robots/software (routine, C1/C4) — its own headline; converges with Felten SMJ (C13); shares patent-text method with Mann-Puttmann (X3) but opposite (adverse) skill conclusion.","provenance_note":"WORKING PAPER ONLY — no peer-reviewed journal version as of 2026-07 (cited from SSRN 3482150 / michaelwebb.co PDF; withdrawn from at least one WP series). DISCOVERY LEAD — do NOT admit to the task-72 JOURNAL-ONLY corpus. Its benign-outcome journal counterpart (Mann-Puttmann X3) IS admissible."},
    {"id":"W2","role":"lead_flagged","doi":"10.1016/S0169-7218(11)02410-5","doi_verified":true,"title":"Skills, Tasks and Technologies: Implications for Employment and Earnings","authors":"Acemoglu, Daron; Autor, David","venue":"Handbook of Labor Economics, Vol. 4B (Elsevier)","type":"book_chapter","year":2011,"pages":"1043-1171","admissible_task72":false,"why_relevant":"The definitive task-framework synthesis; foundational reference behind essentially every canon paper above. A top review will cite it.","what_it_contrasts_with":"n/a — the synthesizing survey, not a contrast.","provenance_note":"HANDBOOK / BOOK CHAPTER, not a journal article — not admissible under the task-72 JOURNAL-ONLY policy. Listed so the reviewer knows the canonical synthesis exists and why it is excluded."}
  ]
}

The citation-graph expansion is complete. All outputs are isolated under `outputs/acquisition_campaign/` (no collision files were touched — they don't exist on disk yet anyway). Here is my return value.

---

**MODALITY 1 — CITATION-GRAPH EXPANSION: complete.** 4,656 deduped journal-article candidates crawled from the 10 seeds via OpenAlex `referenced_works` + `cited_by`, two hops, no full text fetched.

**Deliverable files (all absolute, under `/home/polaris/polaris_project/outputs/acquisition_campaign/`):**
- `candidates.json` (4.9M) — the 4,656 candidates, deduped by DOI, ranked by seed-centrality then citations. Each record: `doi, openalex_id, title, venue, issn_l, year, type, cited_by_count, coverage_cells, primary_cell, tech_terms, work_terms, graph_hops, graph_directions, linked_seeds, n_seed_links, why_relevant`.
- `summary.json` — counts by cell + all stats below.
- `candidates_top80.tsv` — human-readable ranked head.
- `seeds_resolved.json` — the 10 seeds resolved to their JOURNAL versions (OpenAlex id + DOI).
- `expand.py` — the reproducible pipeline (`resolve` → `crawl` → `assemble`); every API call cached under `raw/`. `pool_meta.jsonl`/`pool_edges.json` are the raw crawl cache (scratch, 202M).

**COUNTS BY CELL (the requested deliverable) — all 7 previously-empty cells now have candidates:**
`general 3131 · education 650 · manufacturing 393 · finance 241 · healthcare 236 · transport 91 · agriculture 80 · retail 59`

**Recency (directly fills the "generative-AI turn 2023-2025 missing" gap):** 3,233 candidates are 2023+ (2026: 1,850 · 2025: 743 · 2024: 413 · 2023: 227); only 942 are ≤2019. Forward citations of the seed neighborhood (3,654 of the candidates) are what surface this current literature; hop-2 contributed the majority (2,975 vs hop-1 1,342).

**The modality did the thing keyword search can't:** it recovered Autor-Levy-Murnane "The Skill Content of Recent Technological Change" (QJE 2003, 5,888 cites, linked to 3 seeds) — the brief's flagship example — plus Autor-Dorn "Low-Skill Service Jobs & Polarization" (AER), Acemoglu-Restrepo "Robots and Jobs" (JPE), Graetz-Michaels "Robots at Work" (REStat), and Noy-Zhang's 2023 *Science* generative-AI productivity experiment. 127 candidates link to ≥2 seeds (high centrality).

**Guardrails honored:**
- *Semantic gate, word boundaries* — admission requires BOTH a TECH token (AI/automation/robot/algorithm/ML/technolog…) AND a specific LABOR token (labor/employment/wage/occupation/skill/task/worker/workforce/productivity…), matched with `\b`. Bare "work" is excluded, so "network"/"framework"/"teamwork" can't leak. 7,528 journal papers were dropped as off-topic (verified: they genuinely lack one axis, e.g. "Offshoring…employment in South Korea" = labor, no tech). No ResNet/skin-cancer/European-Heart-Journal false positives in samples.
- *THE LAW / journal-only* — kept only `type∈{article,review}` with a real journal location; added a working-paper/preprint venue denylist so IMF/SSRN/RePEc/arXiv series that OpenAlex mistypes as "journal" are dropped to discovery-lead status (911 non-journal leads excluded). All 4,656 have well-formed DOIs; 0 dups; 0 seed overlap.
- *Generality* — the gate/cells are a data-driven regex table over the contract's topic, not a task-72 hardcode; cell regexes were polysemy-hardened (transport fell 404→91 after removing "drivers of change"/"labor mobility"; finance 431→241 after removing incidental "financial").

**Caveats for the admission/weighting stage (downstream, not here):** (1) I aimed WIDE per instruction — venue quality varies (some low-tier/proceedings-style journals like "Advances in Economics, Management and Political Sciences" passed the journal filter and need a quality/authority gate). (2) A few genuine "AI + knowledge work" papers with no explicit labor token (or missing OpenAlex abstract) were dropped — recoverable only with full text. (3) Acemoglu-2019-JEP has only 8 `referenced_works` in OpenAlex (thin backward reach), but its 2,212 forward citations more than compensated. (4) Nothing here is admitted or provenance-bound — no bytes were fetched; these are candidates for the next modality.

Everything checks out: 299 records, 298 unique DOIs, zero duplicate DOIs, all in-window, all with the required fields. No forbidden files touched (I only wrote under `outputs/acquisition_campaign/` and appended to the shared ledger via the Acquirer; `recency.py` and the profile were used unmodified — `rows` was bumped in memory only).

---

## MODALITY 3 — 2023-2025 FRONTIER: how much journal literature is actually reachable

**Method (faithful to the frontier lane + THE LAW).** Ran `recency.py`'s FRONTIER LANE unmodified: 6 targeted generative-AI×labor query needs (generative AI / LLM / ChatGPT × employment, wages, jobs, occupations, tasks, productivity, skill demand), each expanded into overlapping date-windowed, **date-sorted (never citation-sorted)** bands — `since_boundary` (2023-01-01), `last_12_months`, `online-ahead-of-print`, plus explicit per-calendar-year windows (2023/2024/2025) so a desc-sort sweep doesn't bunch at 2026 — against **Crossref DATE filters + OpenAlex**. Every fetch went through `acquisition.Acquirer` (THE ONE DOOR). **No full text fetched** — metadata + inline abstract only. Admission gate: `journal-article AND (AI term) AND (CORE labor term)`, **word-boundary** regexes (ResNet/skin-cancer/crop-yield papers correctly rejected — AI but no labor anchor); working papers / preprints / proceedings excluded (discovery leads, not journal evidence).

**Headline reachability: abundant, and we are sampling-limited, not supply-limited.**
- **299 deduped on-topic journal articles** admitted (298 unique DOIs): **206 in 2023-2025**, plus **93 in 2026-YTD**.
- Counts by year: **2023=55, 2024=58, 2025=93, 2026=93**.
- **Generative-AI-specific slice: 60** (2023=14, 2024=4, 2025=20, 2026=22). The 2024 dip is a sampling/throttle artifact, not a real trend.
- These 299 came from only `rows=100`/query off the date-sorted top. The raw date-windowed relevance funnels are enormous — **OpenAlex ~23K–31K hits/yr, Crossref ~260K–325K hits/yr** before the on-topic gate — so on-topic 2023-2025 journal literature on AI & work is plentiful and trivially reachable by date; recall here is capped by sampling depth, not scarcity.

**The seven empty coverage cells now have candidate leads** (heuristic keyword tag on title+abstract): education 47, healthcare 39, finance 21, manufacturing 18, transport 12, retail 6, agriculture 2. **Caveat:** `cell_served` is a first-pass tag and fires on incidental mentions — a Columbia tax-law paper got tagged "agriculture," a macro OECD paper "transport." Industry counts are leads to verify, not confirmed domain evidence; agriculture (n=2) is unreliable. healthcare/finance/manufacturing are the more genuine.

**Venue-quality caveat (matters for THE LAW).** The on-topic COUNT is high, but venue quality is heterogeneous. A credible peer-reviewed core exists — *Journal of Applied Psychology*, *Regional Studies*, *Journal of Computer-Mediated Communication*, *Journal of the Association for Information Systems*, "Economics of ChatGPT: a labor-market view on the occupational impact" — but the majority are regional/low-impact venues, and some are dubious (a *Journal of Advanced Zoology* piece on "Impact of ChatGPT on Industry"). Only ~30-40 sit in recognizable mid/high-tier journals. **A venue-authority filter (DOAJ/Scopus/tier gate) is the required next step before any of these enter the corpus.**

**Throttle honesty (THE LAW upheld).** OpenAlex 429'd repeatedly under concurrent shared-IP load from the other live wheels. Those queries are recorded as **THROTTLED**, never as "no evidence" — so the counts above are a **lower bound**. Run-1: 9/90 queries THROTTLED (recorded as such). A 3s-spacing re-run hit a worse 429 storm and was stopped as low-ROI; the reported set is the honest union of what responded.

**Deliverables (all under the allowed dir):**
- `/home/polaris/wt/flywheel/outputs/acquisition_campaign/frontier_2023_2025_FINAL.json` — canonical result: 299 records with `doi/title/venue/year/why_relevant/cell_served` (+ `genai_specific`, matched ai/labor terms, `dbs`, `found_via`, abstract) and all aggregate counts by year/cell/db.
- `/home/polaris/wt/flywheel/outputs/acquisition_campaign/frontier_harvest.py` — harvester (recency.py frontier lane via Acquirer).
- `/home/polaris/wt/flywheel/outputs/acquisition_campaign/run1_frontier.json` — full run with per-query **reachability ceilings** + THROTTLED detail.
- `/home/polaris/wt/flywheel/outputs/acquisition_campaign/merge_runs.py` — union/dedup.

**Recommended next step:** run this record set through a venue-authority/quality gate, then `provenance.py` binding (manifestation_id + content_hash) for the surviving ~30-60 credible journal articles — that is the real 2023-2025 evidence the corpus is missing.

**MODALITY 2 — OA-route discovery for the empty industry cells: COMPLETE.**

**Deliverables (both under the sanctioned `outputs/acquisition_campaign/` only):**
- Results: `/home/polaris/wt/flywheel/outputs/acquisition_campaign/oa_cell_discovery.json` (158 deduped journal hits, each with `doi/title/venue/year/why_relevant/cell_served` + `also_serves/source/cited_by/is_oa/authors/already_in_corpus/found_by_query`; plus `counts_by_cell`, `source_health`, `discovery_leads_nonjournal`, `thin_cell_analysis`, `backend_failures`)
- Runner: `/home/polaris/wt/flywheel/outputs/acquisition_campaign/oa_route_discovery.py` (re-runnable; `--sources openalex` union-merges without re-hitting healthy sources)

**Generality held:** the source set was DERIVED by `source_router.route()` over `config/source_routes.yaml` from the task-72 contract (fired OA index/identity routes: openalex, crossref, doaj, europe_pmc, core, openaire) — not a hardcoded task-72 net. The only task-specific inputs are the per-cell query strings and industry vocabulary.

**Semantic gate (word-boundary, identical rule to `source_router._contains`):** a hit must match AI ∧ work/labor ∧ that-industry. Verified against the exact traps: "neural **network**" never counts as "work" (bare "work" is not a trigger); "ret**ai**l"/"tr**ai**ning"/"dom**ai**n" never count as "ai"; and a livestock **"AI = artificial insemination"** confound ("drone-enabled semen delivery") is rejected. Journal-only enforced; SSRN/arXiv/working-paper containers that Crossref mislabels `journal-article` are held aside as discovery leads, per THE LAW.

**Counts by cell (journal hits | 2023+ | cited≥10):**
- healthcare **45** | 41 | 6 — flagship 10.7861/futurehosp.6-2-94 (Future Healthcare J, cited 1352)
- manufacturing **39** | 31 | 2 — 10.1111/rode.70110 (Review of Development Economics, 2026)
- education **23** | 23 | 4 — 10.3390/buildings15060933 (cited 25)
- transport **19** | 13 | 3 — 10.1111/ntwe.12272 (New Technology, Work and Employment, cited 50)
- agriculture **11** | 6 | 2 — 10.1016/j.agsy.2020.103039 (Agricultural Systems, cited 41)

**Total: 158 unique on-topic journal articles, 156 with DOI, 131 (83%) published 2023+** — directly filling the generative-AI-turn recency gap the corpus was missing. All 158 are NEW (deduped against 707 known-corpus DOIs; 0 overlap). Plus **47 non-journal discovery leads** held aside (finance 10, transport 10, manufacturing 9) as version-pursuit targets.

**Honest thin-cell finding:** no cell is empty — every previously-empty outline cell now carries journal evidence (min 10, max 45). Retail (10), finance (11), agriculture (11) are the THINNER cells. This is an OPERATIONAL floor from 3 of 5 OA sources, **NOT a saturated absence** — it does not license "the literature is thin here." Finance is telling: fewest journal hits but the most SSRN working-paper leads, i.e. its AI-labor evidence skews to non-journal working papers.

**Backend failures (BACKEND_FAILED, never "no evidence" — every attempt went through `acquisition.Acquirer`):**
- **OpenAlex → THROTTLED (429)** on a shared-egress IP after an initial healthy run. It supplied 115 hits in the first pass and would only ADD to these counts (+ citation signal); persistently rate-limited for ~15 min, so it was excluded from the final run rather than hammered. Re-run `--sources openalex` from a cooled IP to union it in.
- **CORE → ACCESS_DENIED (401)** — the `CORE_API_KEY` on disk is rejected under both `?api_key=` and `Authorization: Bearer`. Entitlement, not absence; and CORE's value is full-text fetch (Modality 3), not discovery — its journals are already indexed by the working sources.

No collision: wrote only to `outputs/acquisition_campaign/` with my own filenames; the concurrent frontier wheel uses the shared `event_ledger.jsonl` and its own `frontier_*.json` — untouched. Did not touch `cellcog_composer.py`, `journal_corpus_content.json`, or `evidence_cards_bound.json`. No full text was fetched.
The run is proceeding through Tier 0. I'll let the monitor stream milestones and report when it completes. Waiting for progress events.

I have the complete, byte-verified picture. Every reportable source is bound to `byte_sha256` + `text_sha256`, and the http tally confirms **200×132 / 403×24 / zero 429s** — the barrier is pure access-control, not throttling. Here is the census.

# THE HONEST ADMISSION CENSUS — outputs/acquisition_campaign/

## 0. THE FINDING THAT DOMINATES ALL OTHERS: the run did not run

The fetch worker was **killed ~63 seconds into a 55-minute / 900-unit budget** — no traceback, no `budget_stopped`, no `write_outcomes` (the `fetch_outcomes.json` on disk is stale, from a prior 8-unit/5-min *sample*). Across **both** run segments the ledger holds **16 distinct units, ever — out of a 5,061-unit union (0.3%).** 2,571 units carried an *offline* OA URL that cost zero network and were never spent.

So this is a census of a **barely-started run, not of the access ceiling.** Within the 16 attempted, the ceiling was access; across the corpus, the ceiling is *runtime* — the process needs to actually complete. Both are real, and they are different findings.

All 16 attempted were **Tier-0 empty-cell** targets (the `m2_empty_cells` scout), so what we see is exactly the hardest cells — which is informative.

## 1. Provenance graph over the 16 — artifact_kind/completeness derived FROM THE BYTES

I re-ran the on-disk reducers (`event_ledger.derive_content_profile / _semantic_binding / _route_status`) read-only over the ledger (`outputs/acquisition_campaign/census_probe.py`), then hand-checked every "FULLTEXT" against its actual extracted-text blob. **The reducer over-admits by 3** — two version-mismatch manifestations and one predatory landing page it scores as journal full text:

| unit | reducer says | THE BYTES say | my verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| 10.1016/j.caeai.2025.100476 | FULLTEXT / SAME_WORK / 17,247w | locator = `econstor.eu/.../GLO-DP-1668.pdf`; header = "**Working Paper … GLO Discussion Paper No. 1668 … Global Labor Organization**" | **DISCOVERY LEAD** (working paper, not the Elsevier C&E:AI article) |
| 10.1145/3757409 | FULLTEXT / SAME_WORK / 16,172w | locator = `arxiv.org/pdf/2508.09438`; arXiv watermark in header | **DISCOVERY LEAD** (arXiv preprint, not the ACM/PACMHCI version) |
| 10.70211/bafr.v1i1.178 | FULLTEXT / 1,919w | title+abstract+chrome; tail = "Published by **WISE Pendidikan Indonesia**", predatory Vol 1/Iss 1 | **QUARANTINE** (predatory landing/thin) |

The `caeai` miss is structural, not a fluke: `_PREPRINT_STAMP` (event_ledger.py:381) knows `nber|iza|arxiv:\d|this version:` but **not** "GLO Discussion Paper" or a bare "Working Paper" heading, so `preprint_stamp_in_header=false` and binding defaults to SAME_WORK. The stale `fetch_outcomes.json` already counts this GLO working paper as an "education FETCHED" — i.e. the pipeline *would ship a working paper as a journal full text* today. That is the exact failure THE LAW names ("Acemoglu's robot effect was 0.37pp in the WP and 0.2pp in the JPE").

**ADMISSIBLE-BUT-LOW-QUALITY — mechanically passes, predatory-tier, flagged (1):**

**DISCOVERY LEAD — preprint/working-paper bytes (2):** caeai→GLO-DP-1668; 10.1145/3757409→arXiv:2508.09438.

**QUARANTINE — abstract-only / landing (4):** 10.1111/ntwe.12272 (Cadmus abstract, 293w), 10.36922/aih.4903 (journal landing, 1,089w), 10.1111/ntwe.12282 (EconStor landing, 269w), 10.70211/bafr.v1i1.178 (predatory landing, 1,919w).

**NO CONTENT — transport failure, NOT absence, retriable via another route (4):** 10.1016/j.iref.2025.104292 (Elsevier JS "Redirecting"), 10.1016/j.joitmc.2025.100607 (Elsevier 403+redirect), 10.3390/buildings15060933 (MDPI 403), 10.70445/gjcsai.1.1.2025.43-54 (predatory; process died mid-fetch — this is the last ledger event).

## 3. THE CENSUS

**NEW admissible journal works: 10 → 15** (the five above). **→ 16 only if the predatory IJFMR is admitted.** From 16 attempted units — this is a floor set by a killed run, not a ceiling.

**Empty cells now ≥2 independent admissible works:**
- **healthcare: 4 ✓** — BMC Nursing, Nursing Reports, OFID, Bull WHO. **The only cell that clears the bar.**
- manufacturing / retail / finance / education / agriculture / transport: **0 credible each.** Best case is one *dubious* item per cell (a preprint, a predatory venue, or an abstract). The m2 cell tags are loose keyword co-occurrence — Bull WHO was tagged "finance," PNAS Nexus "healthcare+transport," IJFMR four cells at once; by the bytes none of those hold.
- Structural note: the reason healthcare closed and the others did not is **route, not topic** — PMC/EuropePMC exposes health-sciences full text openly; Wiley/Elsevier/MDPI-direct/ACM/OUP-direct returned 403 or JS walls. And the industrial cells are "empty" partly because credible journals under-cover AI-and-work there, so the keyword net into them disproportionately surfaces predatory venues (BAFR, IJFMR, GJCSAI, AccScience-AIH all appeared here).

**2023-2025 frontier admitted: 5 of 5** (BMC Nursing '24, Nursing Reports '24, OFID '24, Bull WHO '25, PNAS Nexus '24; +IJFMR '24 if counted). The new material is uniformly current — the pre-2023 recency gap is addressed *in kind*, just not *in volume*.

**Genuine CONTRASTS gained (disagree with the seed): ~0.** None of the five numerically contradict Acemoglu/Goos/Damioli/etc. The four healthcare studies are perception/survey evidence in a **new sector** — a thematic counter-note (worker apprehension vs. the seed's productivity-optimism) but not a numeric contrast. PNAS Nexus (2024) is a task-level occupational-exposure study that **extends** Tolan 2021 rather than disputing it. Value added = coverage + recency, not disagreement.

**Honestly unreachable (within the 16):**
- **throttled-retriable (429): 0.** Zero 429/503 anywhere in the ledger. The 24 blocked events are all **403** (Wiley 6, MDPI 5, DOAJ 4, doi.org 3, uni-goettingen 2, OUP 2, ScienceDirect 2) plus Elsevier JS-redirect walls. Nothing here is "wait and retry"; it is publisher access-control.
- **paywalled / no-journal-OA: ~7** — iref, joitmc, caeai (journal version; only the GLO WP was OA), 3757409 (only arXiv was OA), ntwe.12272, ntwe.12282, aih.4903, buildings. The journal manifestation was blocked and the only OA copy was a preprint / working paper / abstract / landing.

Do NOT let the pipeline's own accounting slip in the two discovery leads (caeai/GLO byte `bec169024cb8ba1096967689`; arXiv byte `7f7b62f5e26f6ff8c372e8ec`) — the reducer scores both as SAME_WORK journal full text and they are not.

## Bottom line
Max-effort search did not yield 25, or 60 — it yielded **5** credible new journal works, because **the run was killed at 0.3% of the union**. That is a **runtime/orchestration failure, not an access ceiling.** But the 16 units it did touch expose a sharp, actionable access pattern: **PMC/EuropePMC = 5/5 clean journal full text; every publisher-direct economics/management route (Elsevier, Wiley, MDPI-direct, ACM, OUP-direct) = 403 or JS wall or preprint-only OA.** Re-running to completion is the single highest-value next step (2,571 offline-OA hints are sitting unused and the fetcher is idempotent/resumable); and to reach the non-health cells at all, the corpus will need either the PMC-indexed subset mined deeply or a paid/authenticated route — the free OA copies of those journals are overwhelmingly preprints and abstracts.
